title: Subject-Verb Agreement Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Subject-verb agreement means the verb must match its subject - singular subjects take singular verbs, and plural subjects take plural verbs. Most mistakes happen when the subject is hidden inside a longer phrase, or when a word looks plural but is actually singular (like 'news' or 'mathematics'). Here are 50 of the most common mistakes, explained simply, with an easy way to fix each one.

commonMistakes.wrong: She go to school every day.
commonMistakes.right: She goes to school every day.
commonMistakes.why: In the present simple tense, verbs must end in '-s' or '-es' whenever the subject is he, she, or it. 'She go' skips this rule. To fix it in future, ask yourself: can I replace the subject with he/she/it? If yes, the verb needs an 's' at the end.

commonMistakes.wrong: He don't like coffee.
commonMistakes.right: He doesn't like coffee.
commonMistakes.why: 'Don't' is short for 'do not', which is only used with I/you/we/they. With he/she/it, you must use 'doesn't' (does not). A simple way to remember this: he/she/it always pairs with 'doesn't', never 'don't'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The dogs barks loudly.
commonMistakes.right: The dogs bark loudly.
commonMistakes.why: 'Dogs' is plural, so the verb should not have an 's' - only singular subjects (he/she/it) take an '-s' verb ending. As a rule, plural subjects always take the plain form of the verb, with no extra letters added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Everyone are happy today.
commonMistakes.right: Everyone is happy today.
commonMistakes.why: Words like everyone, everybody, someone, and anybody sound like they mean 'many people', but grammatically they are always treated as one single person, so they take a singular verb. Whenever you see 'every-' or 'some-' combined with 'one/body', use is/was/has, never are/were/have.

commonMistakes.wrong: The team are winning.
commonMistakes.right: The team is winning.
commonMistakes.why: Words describing a group - team, family, class, committee - are treated as one single unit when they act together, so they take a singular verb. Only use the plural form if you are talking about the individual members doing different things separately.

commonMistakes.wrong: Neither of the boys were late.
commonMistakes.right: Neither of the boys was late.
commonMistakes.why: 'Neither' means 'not one of the two', so even though it's followed by a plural noun (boys), the true meaning is singular. Whenever a sentence starts with 'neither of' or 'either of', use a singular verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: The list of items are on the table.
commonMistakes.right: The list of items is on the table.
commonMistakes.why: The real subject here is 'list', not 'items' - the phrase 'of items' is just extra description. A helpful trick: cover up any 'of + noun' phrase with your hand, and match the verb to whatever word is left.

commonMistakes.wrong: Each of the students have a book.
commonMistakes.right: Each of the students has a book.
commonMistakes.why: 'Each' always refers to one person at a time, even when it's followed by a plural noun like 'students'. Whenever a sentence begins with 'each of', treat it as singular and use has/is/was.

commonMistakes.wrong: My family are very supportive.
commonMistakes.right: My family is very supportive.
commonMistakes.why: When you talk about your family as one unit that supports you together, use the singular verb. (In British English, 'family are' is sometimes used when talking about members acting individually, but for learners the safest and most common choice is singular.)

commonMistakes.wrong: Ten dollars are too much for this.
commonMistakes.right: Ten dollars is too much for this.
commonMistakes.why: When an amount of money, time, weight, or distance is being discussed as a single total quantity, English treats it as one idea and uses a singular verb, even though the number looks plural. Ask yourself: am I describing one total amount? If yes, use 'is', not 'are'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The news are very disturbing.
commonMistakes.right: The news is very disturbing.
commonMistakes.why: 'News' ends in 's' and looks plural, but it is actually an uncountable, singular noun in English - there's no such thing as 'one new' or 'two news'. Always pair 'news' with is/was, never are/were.

commonMistakes.wrong: Mathematics are my favorite subject.
commonMistakes.right: Mathematics is my favorite subject.
commonMistakes.why: Subject names that end in '-ics', like mathematics, physics, and economics, refer to one single field of study, so they take a singular verb even though they end in 's'. Remember: the 's' at the end is just part of the word's spelling, not a plural marker here.

commonMistakes.wrong: The number of students are increasing.
commonMistakes.right: The number of students is increasing.
commonMistakes.why: 'The number of' means one specific total, so the true subject is 'the number', which is singular. Compare this with 'a number of', which means 'many' and takes a plural verb - the word right after 'the' or 'a' makes all the difference.

commonMistakes.wrong: A number of students is absent today.
commonMistakes.right: A number of students are absent today.
commonMistakes.why: 'A number of' means 'several' or 'many', so it behaves like a plural quantity word and needs a plural verb. Keep these two phrases straight: 'the number of' = singular, 'a number of' = plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: Everybody have their own opinion.
commonMistakes.right: Everybody has their own opinion.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'everyone', the word 'everybody' refers to a single, general person and is always grammatically singular. Pair it with 'has', not 'have', even though the meaning feels like it includes many people.

commonMistakes.wrong: One of the reasons are cost.
commonMistakes.right: One of the reasons is cost.
commonMistakes.why: The phrase 'one of the...' always has 'one' as its true subject, no matter how many nouns follow it. Whenever you see 'one of the' at the start of a subject phrase, use a singular verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: The committee have decided.
commonMistakes.right: The committee has decided.
commonMistakes.why: Committee, board, and jury describe a single official group making one decision together, so they take a singular verb in standard usage. Think of the group as one voice speaking, not many separate people.

commonMistakes.wrong: Physics are a difficult subject.
commonMistakes.right: Physics is a difficult subject.
commonMistakes.why: Like mathematics, 'physics' names one academic subject, even though it ends in 's'. Words ending in -ics that describe a field of study almost always take a singular verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Neither the manager nor the staff was informed.
commonMistakes.right: Neither the manager nor the staff were informed.
commonMistakes.why: With 'neither...nor' and 'either...or', the verb agrees with whichever noun is closest to it - here that's 'staff', which is being used as a plural. As a rule of thumb, always look at the noun right before the verb to decide singular or plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: The scissors is on the table.
commonMistakes.right: The scissors are on the table.
commonMistakes.why: Tools and clothing items made of two joined parts - scissors, glasses, trousers, pants - are always treated as plural in English, even though they refer to one object. There is no singular verb form for these words; always use 'are/were'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Each and every student are responsible.
commonMistakes.right: Each and every student is responsible.
commonMistakes.why: The phrase 'each and every' emphasizes individuals one at a time, so it always takes a singular verb, just like 'each' alone does. Whenever you see 'each' anywhere before the verb, default to singular.

commonMistakes.wrong: Ten years are a long time.
commonMistakes.right: Ten years is a long time.
commonMistakes.why: When a time period is described as one single stretch or duration, use a singular verb, even though the number is more than one. Ask: is this one block of time, or ten separate years? If it's one idea, use 'is'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The police is investigating the case.
commonMistakes.right: The police are investigating the case.
commonMistakes.why: 'Police' is a special word in English - it has no singular form and is always treated as a plural noun, referring to police officers as a group. Always pair 'police' with are/were/have, never is/was/has.

commonMistakes.wrong: Either of the answers are correct.
commonMistakes.right: Either of the answers is correct.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'neither', the word 'either' means 'one of the two', so it stays singular no matter what noun follows it. Train your ear to hear 'either of' and 'neither of' as singular subjects.

commonMistakes.wrong: The staff is all working overtime.
commonMistakes.right: The staff are all working overtime.
commonMistakes.why: When 'staff' refers to each employee doing their own separate task, rather than one unified group, English switches to a plural verb. This is different from 'staff' used as one unit - context tells you which meaning is intended.

commonMistakes.wrong: Everyone in the class were present.
commonMistakes.right: Everyone in the class was present.
commonMistakes.why: No matter how long the phrase is between 'everyone' and the verb, 'everyone' itself is always singular. Don't let the words 'in the class' distract you - go back to the core subject, 'everyone'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The jury have reached its verdict.
commonMistakes.right: The jury has reached its verdict.
commonMistakes.why: A jury reaching one single verdict is acting as one collective decision-making body, so it takes a singular verb. If the jury members were described as disagreeing separately, plural could be used instead - but for one shared decision, use singular.

commonMistakes.wrong: Both of the answer is correct.
commonMistakes.right: Both of the answers are correct.
commonMistakes.why: 'Both' always refers to two things together, so it needs a plural noun (answers, not answer) and a plural verb (are). Whenever you use 'both', check that both the noun and the verb are plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: There is many reasons for this.
commonMistakes.right: There are many reasons for this.
commonMistakes.why: In 'there is/are' sentences, the verb must match the noun that comes right after it, not the word 'there' itself. Since 'reasons' is plural, the sentence needs 'are', not 'is'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Here's the keys you asked for.
commonMistakes.right: Here are the keys you asked for.
commonMistakes.why: "Here's" is a contraction of 'here is', which is singular, so it shouldn't be used before a plural noun like 'keys'. Before you shorten 'here is' or 'there is', check whether the noun after it is singular or plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: One in five students fail the exam.
commonMistakes.right: One in five students fails the exam.
commonMistakes.why: In phrases like 'one in five', the actual subject is 'one', so the verb must be singular, even though 'students' appears right before the verb. Focus on the very first word of the phrase - 'one' - to decide the verb form.

commonMistakes.wrong: The majority of people thinks this is fair.
commonMistakes.right: The majority of people think this is fair.
commonMistakes.why: 'The majority of' takes its verb form from the noun that follows it - here, 'people' is plural, so the verb must be plural too. When 'the majority of' is followed by a plural noun, always use a plural verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Economics have always interested me.
commonMistakes.right: Economics has always interested me.
commonMistakes.why: Like mathematics and physics, 'economics' refers to one subject area and is treated as singular, even though it ends in 's'. Don't let the final 's' fool you into thinking it needs a plural verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Neither answer are acceptable.
commonMistakes.right: Neither answer is acceptable.
commonMistakes.why: 'Neither' followed directly by a singular noun (answer, not answers) always needs a singular verb. Remember: 'neither answer is', but 'neither of the answers is' - both stay singular.

commonMistakes.wrong: My glasses is broken.
commonMistakes.right: My glasses are broken.
commonMistakes.why: Like scissors and trousers, 'glasses' (meaning spectacles) is always plural in English, even when referring to a single pair. Pair these two-part object words with 'are', never 'is'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Everyone were invited to the party.
commonMistakes.right: Everyone was invited to the party.
commonMistakes.why: Even in the past tense, 'everyone' remains singular, so it must be paired with 'was', not 'were'. This rule doesn't change between tenses - 'everyone' is always singular, in any tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: The group of students was arguing among themselves.
commonMistakes.right: The group of students were arguing among themselves.
commonMistakes.why: Here, the sentence describes the individual students disagreeing with each other separately, not the group acting as one, so the plural verb fits better. When a sentence emphasizes individual actions within a group, plural verbs are often more natural.

commonMistakes.wrong: Both my sister and brother is coming.
commonMistakes.right: Both my sister and brother are coming.
commonMistakes.why: 'Both...and' always joins two subjects into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you see the pattern 'both X and Y', automatically switch to a plural verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: Ten miles are too far to walk.
commonMistakes.right: Ten miles is too far to walk.
commonMistakes.why: A distance described as one single amount takes a singular verb, similar to money and time expressions. Ask yourself: am I talking about ten separate miles, or one total distance? Here it's one idea, so use 'is'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The audience was clapping their hands.
commonMistakes.right: The audience were clapping their hands.
commonMistakes.why: Since each person in the audience is clapping their own hands individually, the sentence describes separate actions, which fits better with a plural verb. This is the opposite case from a group acting as one.

commonMistakes.wrong: None of the food were touched.
commonMistakes.right: None of the food was touched.
commonMistakes.why: 'Food' is an uncountable noun, so when 'none' refers to it, the verb stays singular. Check the noun after 'of' - if it's uncountable (food, water, information), use a singular verb with 'none'.

commonMistakes.wrong: None of the students was absent.
commonMistakes.right: None of the students were absent.
commonMistakes.why: Unlike food, 'students' is a countable plural noun, so 'none of the students' can correctly take a plural verb. With 'none of', let the noun that follows guide whether you use singular or plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: The dog and the cat is playing.
commonMistakes.right: The dog and the cat are playing.
commonMistakes.why: When two separate subjects are joined by 'and', they combine into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you connect two different nouns with 'and', switch the verb to its plural form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Bread and butter are my favorite breakfast.
commonMistakes.right: Bread and butter is my favorite breakfast.
commonMistakes.why: Even though 'and' usually creates a plural subject, when two foods are thought of as one combined dish (like bread and butter, or fish and chips), English treats the pair as singular. Ask: are these really two separate things, or one combined idea? If one idea, use singular.

commonMistakes.wrong: The rich is getting richer.
commonMistakes.right: The rich are getting richer.
commonMistakes.why: 'The + adjective' (the rich, the poor, the elderly) is a common way to describe a whole group of people, and this pattern always takes a plural verb. Whenever 'the' is placed before an adjective to mean 'people who are ___', treat it as plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: What she said were true.
commonMistakes.right: What she said was true.
commonMistakes.why: A whole clause acting as the subject of a sentence (like 'what she said') is treated as one single idea, so it takes a singular verb. When your subject is an entire clause rather than a simple noun, default to the singular verb form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Ten percent of the students is failing.
commonMistakes.right: Ten percent of the students are failing.
commonMistakes.why: With percentages, the verb agrees with the noun that comes after 'of' - here, 'students' is plural, so the verb must be plural. Always check the noun following 'percent of' to decide the correct verb form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Ten percent of the cake are gone.
commonMistakes.right: Ten percent of the cake is gone.
commonMistakes.why: Here, 'cake' is an uncountable noun, so even though the number 'ten percent' looks the same as before, the verb must now be singular. The rule stays consistent: percentages match whatever noun follows 'of'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The information were useful.
commonMistakes.right: The information was useful.
commonMistakes.why: 'Information' is an uncountable noun in English - it has no plural form, so it always takes a singular verb. Never add 's' to 'information' and never use 'were' with it.

commonMistakes.wrong: My advice are to wait.
commonMistakes.right: My advice is to wait.
commonMistakes.why: Like 'information', 'advice' is uncountable, meaning you can't count 'one advice, two advices' - so it always stays singular. Always pair 'advice' with is/was, and never add an 's' to the word itself.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Everyone' always refers to one general person, so it's grammatically singular and takes 'is'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: When a group like 'team' acts as one unit, use a singular verb.

quiz.sentence: The number of students are increasing.
quiz.correction: The number of students is increasing.
quiz.explanation: 'The number of' means one total figure, so the true subject 'the number' is singular.

quiz.question: Neither of the boys ___ (be) late.
quiz.explanation: 'Neither' means 'not one of the two' and is always singular.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Police' has no singular form in English and is always treated as plural.
